extra.reasoning:
Property: never (rec_drug_a AND rec_drug_b).

rec_drug_a analysis: The only rule writing rec_drug_a is rec_a_from_conjunction (priority 50), firing IF (hepatic_impairment ∧ seizure_history ∧ g6pd_deficiency ∧ ckd_dialysis). Otherwise the default sets it false. So rec_drug_a = true exactly when all four of those inputs are true (call this condition K).

rec_drug_b analysis: Two rules write rec_drug_b: guard_suppress_b (priority 90, condition = K, sets false) and rec_b_from_trigger (priority 50, condition = hepatic_impairment, sets true). Default false.

Critical interaction: rec_drug_a can only be true when K holds. Whenever K holds, both rec_drug_b rules are eligible — but guard_suppress_b has priority 90, strictly greater than rec_b_from_trigger's priority 50. Per the conflict-resolution policy (highest-priority matching rule wins per output), guard_suppress_b dominates and sets rec_drug_b := false. The guard's condition is identical to (and thus exactly covers) the condition that makes rec_drug_a true, so there is no input where rec_drug_a is true while the guard fails to fire.

Therefore in every state where rec_drug_a = true, rec_drug_b is forced false; and whenever K is false, rec_drug_a is false (default), making the conjunction trivially false regardless of rec_drug_b. Across all 2^6 = 64 input combinations, rec_drug_a ∧ rec_drug_b is never satisfiable. The mutual-exclusion property holds.
